sources.title: ARIES: A Transaction Recovery Method Supporting Fine-Granularity Locking and Partial Rollbacks Using Write-Ahead Logging (Mohan et al., ACM TODS 1992)
Why Do Computers Stop and What Can Be Done About It? (Jim Gray, Tandem TR 85.7, 1985)
The Recovery Manager of the System R Database Manager (Gray et al., ACM Computing Surveys 1981)
Temporal Platform Documentation — Events and Event History
Temporal Platform Documentation — Workflow Execution overview
Learn Temporal — Develop code that durably executes
Reactor — dynamic, concurrent, dependency-resolving saga orchestrator (Ash project)
Ash — Reactor integration (undo options for Ash steps)
Stripe — Designing robust and predictable APIs with idempotency

structure.audience: A backend engineer who has built workflow tooling on Oban / Ash Reactor and wants the underlying theory of durable execution — the ideas, grounded in concrete engines but not a product tour.
structure.rationale: Start from the failure model (crashes, partitions, lying disks) because every mechanism exists to survive a specific failure. Build the append-only log first (WAL/ARIES lineage) since it is the substrate everything else stands on. Then the three seed concepts in the order they depend on each other: replay reconstructs state from the log; idempotency is what makes replaying side effects safe; sagas handle the failures replay cannot hide by compensating. Add durable timers (a subtle non-determinism source) between idempotency and sagas. Close with a concrete engine comparison (Temporal, Oban, Ash Reactor) and an honest accounting of what is actually guaranteed vs. what is illusion.

claims.text: Durable execution assumes a fail-stop / crash-recovery model: processes crash and later restart, networks drop and reorder messages, and disks can return stale or torn data — every mechanism exists to survive one of these.

Jim Gray's 1985 analysis found most production system failures came from software and operations, not hardware; the design response is to make the system able to fail cleanly and recover, not to prevent all failure.
Durability means a committed effect survives a crash; the durable record must reach stable storage before the process is allowed to report success.
Durable execution reframes an ordinary program so its progress — not just its data — is persisted, so that after a crash the program resumes from where it was rather than restarting from the top.

pitfalls: Assuming a retry is free — a retried side effect that is not idempotent double-charges.
Confusing availability (staying up) with durability (surviving a crash with committed work intact).

claims.text: Write-ahead logging (WAL) requires that the log record describing a change reach stable storage before the changed data page is written, so recovery always has a record of every in-flight change.

Every ARIES log record carries a unique, monotonically increasing Log Sequence Number (LSN); each data page stores the LSN of the last change applied to it, letting recovery tell which changes a page already reflects.

ARIES recovers in three passes: Analysis (rebuild the state at crash time), Redo (repeat history to restore all pages to their crash-time state, including uncommitted changes), then Undo (roll back the transactions that were still in flight).
'Repeating history' — redoing even uncommitted work before undoing it — is the ARIES insight that makes recovery restartable: a crash during recovery just re-runs the same idempotent passes.
The log is the ground truth: the durable, append-only, ordered record of what happened; the materialized state is a cache that can always be rebuilt by replaying the log.

snippets.code:
LSN 41  BEGIN        txn=7
LSN 42  UPDATE       txn=7  acct=A  -100
LSN 43  UPDATE       txn=7  acct=B  +100
LSN 44  COMMIT       txn=7
snippets.note: WAL: the log record is forced to stable storage before the page write; COMMIT is durable once its record is on disk.

pitfalls: Writing the data page before its log record — a crash in between loses the ability to redo or undo the change.

claims.text: Event sourcing persists the sequence of events that changed state rather than the current state; the current state is derived by folding the events.
A Temporal workflow execution is represented as a durable event history (WorkflowStarted, ActivityTaskScheduled, ActivityTaskCompleted, TimerFired, ...) held on the server and never lost.

On recovery a Temporal worker replays the workflow code from the beginning against the event history; the code re-executes but side-effecting activities are not re-run — their recorded results are handed back from history.

Deterministic replay requires the workflow to produce the same commands in the same order given the same history; it must not read wall-clock time, random numbers, or external state directly, since those would differ between the original run and the replay.

Changing workflow code between the original run and a replay can make replay diverge from history (a non-determinism error); engines address this with versioning/patching so old histories keep replaying the old path.

snippets.code:
history:  Started -> Activity(charge)=ok -> Timer(24h) fired -> Activity(ship)=ok
replay:   run code; at charge -> use recorded ok (do NOT re-charge)
                    at timer  -> already fired, continue
                    at ship   -> use recorded ok
snippets.note: Replay reconstructs in-memory state from history without repeating committed side effects.

pitfalls: Calling Date.now()/rand()/DB reads directly inside workflow code breaks determinism — route them through the engine so the value is recorded in history.
A checkpoint stores state; an event log stores the deltas — replaying a log from a checkpoint is cheaper than replaying from zero.

claims.text: Exactly-once delivery is not achievable over an unreliable network; the practical construction is at-least-once delivery plus idempotent processing, which yields an exactly-once effect.

An idempotency key is a client-generated unique token attached to a request; the server records the outcome under that key and returns the stored result on any retry, so the operation takes effect at most once.

Stripe stores the status code and body of the first request for a given idempotency key — whether it succeeded or failed — and replays that saved response on retries; it recommends V4 UUIDs or similarly high-entropy keys.
For the guarantee to hold, the idempotency key and the business effect must be committed in the same atomic transaction; otherwise a crash between them reopens the double-apply window.

Durable-execution engines lean on idempotency because at-least-once activity delivery means an activity can run more than once (e.g. after a worker crash before the result is recorded); the activity, or the system it calls, must tolerate the repeat.

snippets.code:
POST /charges   Idempotency-Key: 5f2b...
  first time  -> perform charge, store {key -> (200, body)} in same txn
  retry       -> key found -> return stored (200, body); do NOT charge again

pitfalls: Naturally-idempotent operations (set x=5) need no key; accumulating ones (add 100) do.
An idempotency key with no expiry grows unbounded; too short an expiry reopens the retry window.

claims.text: A durable timer is recorded in the log as an event with a fire time, so a 'sleep for 30 days' survives crashes, deploys, and worker restarts — the wait is state, not a blocked thread.

Reading the wall clock directly inside deterministic workflow code is non-deterministic; the engine instead records the timer's firing as a history event so replay sees the same fire, not a fresh clock read.

Long-running activities report liveness with periodic heartbeats; if heartbeats stop within a heartbeat timeout the engine treats the activity as failed and can retry it, distinguishing a slow task from a dead worker.

In queue-based engines like Oban, the equivalent of a durable timer is a scheduled job with a run-at timestamp persisted in the database, and cron/periodic jobs the queue makes available at their due time.

pitfalls: A durable sleep is not a thread sleep — the worker is free during the wait and the timer lives in the store.
Retry backoff and timeouts are themselves durable timers; misconfigured, they either give up too soon or hammer a downstream forever.

claims.text: A saga (Garcia-Molina & Salem, 1987) models a long-lived transaction as a sequence of smaller transactions T1..Tn, each with a compensating transaction C1..Cn; the system guarantees either all Ti complete, or a prefix runs and its compensations undo it.

Sagas exist because holding ACID locks across a long-lived transaction blocks everyone else; a saga releases locks after each step and gives up global isolation in exchange, accepting that intermediate states are visible.

A compensating transaction Ci semantically undoes Ti but does not necessarily restore the exact prior state — it restores an acceptable approximation (e.g. a refund offsets a charge; it does not erase that the charge happened).

If a saga step Ti fails, compensations are run in reverse order (Ci-1, Ci-2, ... C1) for the steps that already committed; compensations should themselves be idempotent because they too can be retried after a crash.

Reactor (Ash project) is a dependency-resolving saga orchestrator: each step may define compensate/4 (called if the step itself fails, to recover/retry/clean up) and undo/4 (called on previously-successful steps when a later step fails unrecoverably).

snippets.note: A trip-booking saga: each booking has a cancellation as its compensation.

pitfalls: Compensation is semantic, not physical rollback — some effects (an email sent) cannot be un-sent, only offset.
A compensation can fail too; it must be retryable and idempotent, or the saga gets stuck half-undone.

claims.text: Temporal implements durable execution by event-sourcing the workflow: deterministic workflow code + a server-held event history + at-least-once activities whose results are recorded and replayed.
Oban is a Postgres/SQLite/MySQL-backed job queue for Elixir; jobs are rows, execution is at-least-once, and completed jobs are retained for inspection so work is not lost across crashes.

Oban enforces uniqueness at insert time across args/queue/worker/period; the open-source uniqueness check is not backed by a DB unique constraint and can race, whereas Oban Pro's Smart Engine uses unique constraints for stronger guarantees.
Oban Pro Workflows link jobs into a DAG; a job waits until all its upstream dependencies complete, enabling sequential, fan-out, and fan-in patterns with dependency resolution that survives retries.

Reactor is an in-process saga orchestrator (compensation/undo, concurrency, dependency resolution); for durability across crashes it is paired with Oban, and AshOban adds periodic 'triggers' and cron 'scheduled actions' over Ash resources.

The three occupy different layers: Temporal persists an entire program's control flow as history; Oban persists units of work as durable queue rows; Reactor structures a single transaction's steps and their compensations in memory and leans on Oban for durability.

pitfalls: 'Durable' is a property of where progress is stored: Temporal in event history, Oban in DB rows, Reactor only once backed by Oban — Reactor alone does not survive a node crash mid-run.

claims.text: The honest guarantee of these systems is at-least-once execution with an exactly-once effect when steps are idempotent — not exactly-once delivery, which is impossible over an unreliable channel.

Durable execution guarantees progress is not lost and will resume, not that any single side effect runs exactly once; the exactly-once-ness is supplied by idempotency, which is the caller's responsibility.
Determinism is a precondition, not a gift: replay only reconstructs state correctly if the workflow avoids hidden non-determinism (clocks, randomness, unrecorded external reads).
Sagas trade isolation for liveness: they guarantee eventual all-or-compensated completion but expose intermediate states, so they are the right tool only when semantic compensation is acceptable.

State survival is bounded by the durability of the store the log lives in (its replication and fsync guarantees); a durable-execution engine is exactly as durable as the database or event store underneath it.

pitfalls: Reading 'exactly-once' on a marketing page as delivery rather than effect.
Assuming determinism holds automatically instead of designing for it.

volatile: Oban / Oban Pro version numbers and whether OSS uniqueness gains DB-constraint backing
Temporal SDK API names for timers/heartbeats/versioning
Reactor and AshOban API surface (compensate/4, undo/4, trigger DSL)
